summary:
The decolonization of Africa was one of the most transformative political processes of the twentieth century, in which the African continent's colonial territories achieved independence from European rule — primarily between 1956 and 1975. At the dawn of the twentieth century, virtually the entire continent had been partitioned among Britain, France, Belgium, Portugal, and other European powers following the 1884 Berlin Conference. Within a generation after [[event:112|World War II]], most of that colonial order had been dismantled.

The momentum for independence grew from multiple sources. African soldiers who had fought for European empires in both world wars returned home with heightened political consciousness and a sense that colonial subjugation was untenable. The [[event:2663|United Nations]] Charter's affirmation of self-determination provided a legal and moral framework. Pan-African ideologies spread by figures such as Kwame Nkrumah and movements like the [[event:3660|pan-Africanism]] current demanded sovereignty. Meanwhile, Britain and France — weakened by war and facing nationalist resistance — increasingly calculated that controlled decolonization was preferable to protracted conflict.

The pace accelerated dramatically in 1960, dubbed the "[[event:3466|Year of Africa]]", when seventeen nations gained independence in a single year. British-controlled territories such as Ghana (1957) and Nigeria (1960) transitioned relatively peacefully, though exceptions existed. France's withdrawal was generally swifter than that of Portugal, which fought desperate colonial wars in Angola, Mozambique, and Guinea-Bissau until its own [[event:3468|Carnation Revolution]] in 1974 ended the old regime.

Some transitions were bitterly violent. The [[event:2709|Algerian War]] (1954–1962) cost over a million lives as France fought a brutal counterinsurgency before finally granting independence. The [[event:3464|Mau Mau Uprising]] in Kenya involved mass detentions and torture. Belgian withdrawal from the Congo in 1960 precipitated the [[event:3357|Congo Crisis]], a prolonged conflict involving UN intervention, secession attempts, and Cold War proxy competition.

The newly independent states inherited colonial borders that had been drawn without regard for ethnic, linguistic, or cultural divisions, creating the seeds of future conflict. The [[event:3661|Organisation of African Unity]] was founded in 1963 to coordinate the continent's governments and promote solidarity, though it largely adhered to the principle of maintaining inherited borders. Southern Africa proved the most resistant to decolonization: apartheid South Africa remained under white minority rule until 1994, Zimbabwe (Rhodesia) until 1980, and Namibia until 1990.
